formatDefinitions.displayName: Complete with words from the box

exercises.title: A: Gap Fill (One Word)
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: The young comedian completely stole the (1) ................ at the city's annual comedy festival.
Stage Review Weekly described their debut performance as nothing short of '(2) ................ of this world'.
items.explanation: Out of this world → **out** of this world.

items.sentence: Devoted fans of Phoenix Rising will be relieved to hear the cast have patched things (3) ................ and are preparing a brand (4) ................ season.

The two opening acts, Dream Weaver and Echoes of Yesterday, don't particularly (5) ................ out as remarkable, but (6) ................ its best, the show contains some of the most breathtaking scenes Phoenix Rising has ever staged.
items.explanation: Not remarkable / at its best → **stand** out, **at** its best.

items.sentence: There's no question they've got physical theatre (7) ................ to a fine art, and when they truly nail it, they nail it (8) ................ style.
items.explanation: Mastered an art → got **down** to a fine art, **in** style.

items.sentence: ★★★ definitely check it (9) ................
★★★★ takes (10) ................ of place in any serious theatre-lover's diary

exercises.title: B: Key Word Transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence:
There's hardly any difference between jealousy and envy. **line**
There's ...... between jealousy and envy.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is very little difference, so the correct answer is "a fine line".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "a fine line", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
I genuinely mean every word of support I'm offering you right now. **bottom**
I am supporting you ...... right now.
items.answer: from the bottom of my heart
acceptedAnswers: from the bottom of my heart

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is sincerely, so the correct answer is "from the bottom of my heart".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
As far as I'm aware, nothing unusual has been reported about the incident. **best**
To ...... , nothing unusual has been reported about the incident.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is as far as I know, so the correct answer is "the best of my knowledge".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "the best of my knowledge", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence:
Perhaps I haven't stated this precisely enough – I will not be attending the conference. **clear**
Perhaps I didn't ...... – I will not be attending the conference.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is express clearly, so the correct answer is "make myself clear".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "make myself clear", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "make myself clear" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.

items.sentence:
Sandra is struggling to manage all the responsibilities she has recently taken on. **top**
All the responsibilities Sandra has recently taken on ...... her.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is overwhelmed by, so the correct answer is "are getting on top of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "are getting on top of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
I have absolutely nothing to regret about my decision, Thomas. **clear**
I ...... about my decision, Thomas.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is no regrets, so the correct answer is "have a clear conscience".

wrongOptions.a clear conscience: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "have a clear conscience", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
The inspectors are committed to discovering the full truth about what occurred that evening. **bottom**
The inspectors are committed to ...... what occurred that evening.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is find the truth, so the correct answer is "get to the bottom of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "get to the bottom of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
I'm sorry to say the patient's condition deteriorated significantly yesterday. **turn**
I'm sorry to say the patient's condition ...... yesterday.
items.answer: took a turn for the worse
acceptedAnswers: took a turn for the worse

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is worsened, so the correct answer is "took a turn for the worse".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Present perfect does not combine with finished past-time words like yesterday or last week.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past simple is used for finished actions at a specific past time.

exercises.title: C: Word Forms (Phrasal Verbs)
exercises.instructions: Complete using the words in the box in the correct form. You will not need one of the words.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete using the words in the box in the correct form. You will not need one of the words.

items.sentence: Bookings are expected to ...... up considerably once the new season begins.
The motorbike was so severely damaged that the insurance company had to ...... it off.
items.explanation: Declare a total loss → **write** it off.

items.sentence: If the council continues to ...... this neighbourhood down, local businesses will eventually close.
items.explanation: Neglect an area → **runs** this neighbourhood down.

items.sentence: There's a tiny scratch on the painting's surface, but the restorer can ...... that up with ease.
Graduates quickly find themselves ...... up their wardrobe when they start their first professional job.
items.explanation: Dress more formally → **smartening** up their wardrobe.

items.sentence: The seminar started rather slowly, but the room ...... up considerably once the guest speaker arrived.
After barely revising, I knew I'd either fail completely or just ...... through by the skin of my teeth.
The forecast says it will be overcast until midday, but the sky should ...... up towards evening.

exercises.title: D: Word Formation
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals.

items.sentence: The diamonds in the display case looked stunning, but they turned out to be entirely ...... (ART) .
She placed her job offer in the local paper's ...... (CLASS) section to attract suitable candidates.
My colleague is such a ...... (PERFECT) that she rewrites every report at least three times before submitting it.
The earthquake left a trail of ...... (DESTROY) across several villages in the mountain region.
Losing the championship in her debut year only ...... (STRONG) her determination to train even harder.
The painting hanging in the lobby isn't an original masterpiece – it's a high-quality ...... (IMITATE) .
Guests are strongly advised to keep their ...... (VALUE) in the secure deposit box provided in each room.
Building ...... (GOOD) with clients takes years of consistent service, but it can be lost through a single mistake.

items.sentenceBefore: James had wiped every surface twice, yet his aunt still managed to spot a single

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "speck" — tiny particle.

wrongOptions.A: "scrap" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "speck" is correct.
wrongOptions.scrap: "scrap" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "speck" is correct.
wrongOptions.B: "gust" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "speck" is correct.
wrongOptions.gust: "gust" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "speck" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "blade" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "speck" is correct.
wrongOptions.blade: "blade" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "speck" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Time expressions like for, since, already, and yet are strong present-perfect signals.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links past actions to now — look for markers like for, since, already, yet, or ever.

items.sentenceAfter: of people had gathered outside the station, though nobody seemed to know exactly why.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "number" — countable noun.

wrongOptions.A: "amount" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "number" is correct.
wrongOptions.amount: "amount" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "number" is correct.
wrongOptions.B: "deal" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "number" is correct.
wrongOptions.deal: "deal" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "number" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "plenty" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "number" is correct.
wrongOptions.plenty: "plenty" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "number" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Eliminate options that break grammar first, then check which remaining word fits the meaning.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "number" is the only option that matches both meaning and register in this sentence.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "few" — negative meaning with plural noun.

wrongOptions.B: "a few" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "few" is correct.
wrongOptions.a few: "a few" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "few" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Time expressions like for, since, already, and yet are strong present-perfect signals.
